questions.question: Why did Mask R-CNN replace RoIPool with RoIAlign?
options: RoIAlign is significantly faster on GPUs than RoIPool
RoIPool was covered by a third-party patent
RoIPool only functions correctly on CPU tensors

RoIPool rounds box coordinates to integers at multiple steps, misaligning the feature map from input pixels by up to a full feature-map pixel; RoIAlign uses bilinear sampling with no rounding, preserving precise localisation
questions.explanation: RoIPool's coordinate rounding accumulates up to a stride-sized misalignment (e.g. 32 pixels on a stride-32 feature map). RoIAlign samples at exact float coordinates via bilinear interpolation. The change lifted mask AP by 3-4 points on COCO in the original paper and is now standard in every detector that requires accurate localisation.

questions.question: In `roi_align_single`, box coordinates are multiplied by `spatial_scale` (e.g. 1/16) before the sampling grid is computed. A box corner is at image pixel (80, 64). What feature-map coordinate does the code compute before the 0.5 alignment offset is subtracted?
options: 80, 64 — no scaling is applied before grid construction
5.0, 4.0 — multiplying by 1/16 maps image pixels to the feature-map grid: 80/16 = 5, 64/16 = 4
1280, 1024 — the spatial scale multiplies coordinates upward
4, 3 — floor division discards the fractional part
questions.explanation: spatial_scale = 1 / stride converts image-space coordinates to feature-map space: 80 × (1/16) = 5.0 and 64 × (1/16) = 4.0. The 0.5 is then subtracted for half-pixel alignment, giving sampling points at 4.5 and 3.5. No integer rounding occurs at any step — that is the essential difference from RoIPool.

questions.question: Mask R-CNN uses per-pixel binary cross-entropy for `L_mask` on the 28×28 output, not Dice loss. Why is BCE sufficient here when whole-image segmentation often requires Dice?
options: Each RoI crop tightly centres the proposed object, so foreground and background pixels within the 28×28 window are far more balanced than in a full-image scene; BCE converges stably without Dice's scale-free overlap correction
Dice loss is unavailable in PyTorch's standard loss library
BCE is always superior to Dice in any 2D binary prediction task
28×28 crops are too spatially small for Dice to compute meaningful gradients
questions.explanation: Dice loss solves the class-imbalance problem that arises when 99% of pixels are background. A 28×28 RoI crop of a proposed box contains a high fraction of foreground pixels by construction, so the imbalance is mild. BCE produces clean gradients in that setting, and the mask head benefits from its simplicity rather than needing Dice's ratio-based overlap term.

questions.question: The `build_custom_maskrcnn` function is called with `num_classes=5` for a dataset that has 4 foreground categories. Why 5 and not 4?
options: A dummy padding class is required for numerical stability in the softmax
The box predictor weight matrix requires an even or power-of-2 output size

Class 0 is always reserved for background; foreground categories start at 1, so a 4-class dataset needs num_classes = 1 (background) + 4 (foreground) = 5 total logits in both the box predictor and mask predictor
5 is a hardcoded default and calling with 4 silently maps to 5
questions.explanation: Faster R-CNN and Mask R-CNN classify each RoI into (C + 1) classes: background plus C foreground classes. The background class (id 0) gives the classifier an explicit 'no object here' logit rather than relying on uniformly low confidence. Forgetting to add 1 for background is the most common fine-tuning shape mismatch bug.

questions.question: You fine-tune Mask R-CNN on a 500-image dataset and val mAP plateaus while train loss keeps dropping. What is the first thing to try?
options: Run for more epochs to give the model more time
Switch to a U-Net for semantic segmentation instead
Freeze the backbone and FPN so the model only fine-tunes the RPN and heads; 500 images is too few to update 23M backbone parameters without overfitting
Raise the learning rate to escape the plateau
questions.explanation: On small datasets there is not enough signal to fine-tune every parameter. Freezing the pretrained backbone and FPN (ImageNet + COCO features) and training only the RPN objectness head and the two classifier/mask heads is the standard recipe in `freeze_backbone_and_fpn` and usually closes the train/val gap in one run.

questions.question: The FPN inside Mask R-CNN has four levels (P2, P3, P4, P5) at strides 4, 8, 16, 32. Why not just use one feature level for all proposals?
options: Four FPN levels are needed to saturate GPU memory for efficient batching
Using one level reduces parameter count, so multiple are required for capacity
Four levels is an arbitrary choice the torchvision authors made for consistency

Objects in natural images span a wide range of scales; routing each proposal to the FPN level matching its size gives the head a feature map with the right receptive field for that object, which is strictly better than forcing all scales through one map
questions.explanation: A small object on a stride-32 feature map occupies a single cell with almost no spatial information. FPN routes small objects to the high-resolution shallow levels (P2) and large objects to the low-resolution deep levels (P5), matching receptive field to object scale. This scale-routing is why every modern detector — YOLOv8, RT-DETR, Mask2Former — uses a feature pyramid.
